feed.title: aqc
feed.subtitle: the personal website of april qiu cheng

entry.title: hexagon corkboard
entry.content:
two long chains, two bars, silver, black. a V on my breast to dress a pretty man

a gift from my mother for my 18th birthday my adolescent love, burned into collarbone

pins   with
       nothing

nothing at all

a ring on a string from a city in the flavor of summer:
                    ice cream & honey toast

      a hollow left wing–someone has carved her out like origami

      an angel forsaken because he is too golden

                  a kaleidoscope of universes, fluttering hurricanes–


              a polaroid of a familiar face
entry.summary: a piece i'm writing for mit's infinite magazine. there's a hexagon corkboard on which i hang my favorite accessories

entry.title: sunflower leather
entry.content:
there was a lost child at the county fair, legs dangling off of a huge, white-painted lawnchair the size of a small car. you were beside her as she spun the clouds floating in the blue june sky into her cotton candy dreams. she had a smile as wide as a bicycle wheel.

i’d like to say that she was a sunflower in a bright summer dress. but we’re not like that, are we? we paint ourselves black because we fear we will melt like candles in the gentle sun. we bury our cores. we bleed and hide the stains in darkness. so in inked wings and red hair and a heavy leather jacket, i mourned the death of the person i was.

but that day, at the top of a giant wheel, the world was beneath our hearts, and i bared my soul to the setting sun. you loved me, in that brief eternity. you held me like the sky. if only we could fly into the sun together instead of saying goodbye–i a white dress ablaze and you an ocean of kind tears;

but i stayed planted to the earth. i bloom because i am still alive. i am a sunflower in a leather jacket. i am twenty years old.
entry.summary: a piece i'm writing for mit's infinite magazine about a summer day in 2022

entry.title: gender, identity, and physics
entry.content:
today, as a college sophomore, i have a decent grasp on my identity. i am chinese-american. i am a woman (although i use that term loosely). i am queer. i am a physicist(-in-training), a double major in physics and math.

back in high school, however, not only was i deep in the closet, i also couldn’t see myself as a physicist, or physicist-in-training, or any kind of physics or math person. i was a girl. i knew i was a girl because everywhere i went, i couldn’t see myself in anyone else. at the physics club at school, at the after school math circle, and even in the online video game spaces that i logged into late at night, it felt as though i had a giant sticky note on my back that screamed “GIRL”. no one thought it was my fault, but nonetheless the poorly hidden condescension and occasional malicious comment gave me the dreaded feeling that for some reason, i was not supposed to be there. for a while back then, i tried to hide this metaphorical sticky note of sorts; i insisted to myself that i didn’t like girly things, that i was a girl but not like the other girls, because i like physics and math and video games. my own parents reinforced this idea. so despite my best efforts, the label stayed plastered on.

the problem is, and always was, about identity. i could never see myself in my peers, let alone the smart upperclassmen that i admired or the brilliant physicists that i’d read about. i idolized and envied those who pursued physics because i was not them, and it viscerally felt as though i could never be. i was someone who tried to do physics. i was a girl.

so, what happened between now and then? many things, including a lot of soul searching, reflections on my identity, and acknowledgement of my passion for astrophysics. progress was non-linear, but slowly, i began to identify myself. i think the primary catalyst was meeting more people like me, i.e. physicists who don’t “look like physicists”, whatever that means. that is, seeing more diversity in physics. yes, diversity is about being equitable, offsetting unfair disadvantages, and reducing the effects of malicious positive feedback loops. on the individual level, however, diversity is about not having to choose between who you are and who you want to be. no one should ever have to lose themselves to follow their dreams.

in the present, i feel liberated from my gender identity. being female is no longer an unwanted label; it is a label i choose to have, and it is a label i occasionally choose to not have–just as i am slowly choosing to identify myself with those who study physics, even though my mom says i don’t look like them. and honestly, that’s quite alright with me
entry.summary: a piece about what gender and diversity mean to me in relation to my academic interests

entry.title: eight-count
entry.content:
one.
above me, the blank canvas is a distant heaven;
i count its ripples like stars

two.
around me, the sky-blue walls are a ladder;
i climb up with sticky hands

three.
behind me, a cold breeze; the window is open
but i am not a dove

four.
in the corner, a mirror shows my worth. lace, gift-wrapped
soft clay ham and a hole

five.
by my side, some body familiar

six.
the yellow ceiling-light glares down like a dentist

seven.
on the wall, the centipede clock crawls,

a faithless metronome
entry.summary: a poem about my body and staring at the ceiling

entry.title: age
entry.content:
i was wandering down the stairs
going somewhere (anywhere)
then, i don’t know when,
i started running and now
with every step that i debase myself
my chalk-white bones
crumble and the perfume-foul flesh
that kisses them melts away
a little bit like candle wax.
hey you, i’m in a hurry, don’t
get in my way—soon, my
curtain skin will rise
i will burn out spectacularly!
and when i finally sink into the
quiet Earth, my skeleton
a beautifully broken doll,
my jaw will distend sideways
in the warm hearth of my
grave, in mock surprise.
entry.summary: a poem about...growing up? going somewhere? doing something? dying? idk but i like this poem

entry.title: sunrises and sunsets
entry.content:
i overheard someone characterizing [redacted mit group] people as people who’ve seen more sunrises than sunsets. i thought this was pretty deep; sunrises are beautiful beginnings, a medal won, a trigger for a new passion, a skip of a heartbeat as you fall in love; sunsets are the light before darkness, a memory of the last time i was happy, a heartbreak, a catharsis of everything crashing down. but sunsets are just as beautiful as sunrises, are they not? i ought to appreciate all the sunsets in my life, and even the darkness after, and the prickling pinpoints of light in the night. but then, i often see sunsets and rarely sunrises (because i don’t wake up early), so maybe i ought to mix things up a bit so i can catch some sunrises from time to time.

anyway, i had this whole train of thought, and then she clarified that she meant that [redacted mit group] people just have insane sleep schedules

(i’m writing this very tired idk how coherent this is)
entry.summary: are you a sunset or sunrise kind of person?

entry.title: the beggar
entry.content:
The cars come to a stop
Gently, or with a screech.
I see a palette.

Red.

Could you spare me a second,
And some change?
Where are you going,
And where will you go?

Some of them are kind.
They roll down the window,
and I can add another book to the shelf.
All things come and go.

Green.

The cars start again
In silence, or with a rumble.
I wave; there’ll be more.

But if I had known that would be the last time I would see you,
I would have said goodbye.
entry.summary: one of the first poems i've written, written at the end of high school at the start of the pandemic, about people and saying goodbye

entry.title: a quote from my favorite short story

link.title: a quote from my favorite short story

entry.content:
i’ve read “the snows of kilimanjaro” several times, and i think its themes really touch the core of who i am. i am not a sellout

Now he would never write the things that he had saved to write until he knew enough to write them well. Well, he would not have to fail at trying to write them either. Maybe you could never write them, and that was why you put them off and delayed the starting. Well he would never know, now.

But, in yourself, you said that you would write about these people; about the very rich; that you were really not of them but a spy in their country; that you would leave it and write of it and for once it would be written by someone who knew what he was writing of. But he would never do it, because each day of not writing, of comfort, of being that which he despised, dulled his ability and softened his will to work so that, finally, he did no work at all.

and there is beauty in chasing something spectacular and fantastical that only i can see; i romanticize the tragic artist

Kilimanjaro is a snow-covered mountain 19,710 feet high, and is said to be the highest mountain in Africa. Its western summit is called the Masai "Ngje Ngi," the House of God. Close to the western summit there is the dried and frozen carcass of a leopard. No one has explained what the leopard was seeking at that altitude.
entry.summary: from "the snows of kilimanjaro" by ernest hemingway
